item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thought this was gonna be a Factory or Broken God SCP at first. Still good though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems really underrated, I kindof hope it gets event featured or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Observing staff (minus the user) were rendered permanently blind.

Well, at least we have SCP-427.

item.title: Re: Getcher facts straight
item.content:encoded: How are you figuring that a robot's going to have a harder time sticking a fresh electrode in a clamp than it would loading (and especially feeding) a new spool? Replacing an electrode is a mechanically simpler procedure and requires less fine motor control.

item.title: Getcher facts straight
item.content:encoded:
One nitpick:
A robot of any type would use a mig or "Wire Feed" welder, rather than an arc welder.
An arc welder requires the ability to replace the used electrodes, among many other issues, things that a robot has difficulty doing. A mig welder uses a spool of wire as a consumable electrode, which is easy for a robot to handle.

And please do not try to justify the usage of arc welders, it just does not fit.

Other than that, this is a nice article.
I look forward to more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, so shiny…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote. Made a minor edit.

How's this fit in with the Grandfather Clock? Any resemblence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like it. It's a complex automated clockwork factory, and there's really not much interesting about it. At least not in what I read, because I quickly lost interest in someone listing off their unnecessarily detailed steampunk wet dream.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kept me reading, it's pretty nice work in my opinion (keep in mind I'm a newb, so my opinion doesn't matter much). Upvoted. Also, I'm wondering: Does the Church of the Broken God know about this? If they do, as the saying goes, moths to a flame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, this has been done before, but it's been done particularly well in this instance. Hesitant +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I'm removing downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted, will remove.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's actually write it down instead of just IRC talk.
Addendum 1713-02 contains next to no information, and its tone is as unprofessional as it gets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For all that there's just a whole ton of words in this, it never stopped being interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can make it more obvious by including a note about authorized clearance levels in the click-through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me, the idea works really well, although it could probably be even better if trimmed down a little, it's suffering from Danielewski's Syndrome in some places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is a perfectly example of why you DO NOT USE OFFSITE LINKS. By the time I got around to this, one of the five images had already been the victim of link rot and had to be removed; the others have been uploaded locally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it. That's a +1. (it's long as Ramadan, though.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn Dwemer ruins

I like it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a minor defense of the inconsistent redaction, I feel obliged to note that the head's description is in an addendum. In-universe, this addendum (which is useless for containment procedures) may only be available to a higher clearance level than those who are actually charged with maintaining Site-71.

If this was the intent though, the relevant clearance levels should made more obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this overall. It could use some polish as noted by others, but it is quite good otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of work obviously went into this. It's not bad, but it needs a little polishing and I have a few questions.

-As noted, it's not very clear where the factory is getting its raw materials from - if they're mining it on their own it needs to be mentioned that the Foundation is trying to locate the source, and if the Foundation is providing it with raw material, they should probably stop.

"Babbage-style engines" doesn't sound like the kind of term that belongs in an article, since the way these things are described doesn't really resemble a difference engine.

Pipes filled with a water-like fluid

Either say "water", or say "an unknown fluid". "A water-like fluid" is imprecise and doesn't give the reader any useful information.

The radiation dosage at the core icosahedral structure has been measured in excess of 50 sieverts/hour… and personnel are not to remain on this level for any longer than two hours - radiation dosimeter tags must be worn at all times to keep track of exposure time.

8 sieverts is a guaranteed lethal dose. Forget two hours, they'll be lucky to last 10 minutes down there.

Objects include toys, weapons, personal vehicles, animals, implants, and, in one case, an artificial clockwork human head that could [REDACTED]… . A wind-up key was located at the base of the skull. When the key was turned, the head began to speak, mentioning items such as how to create an effective lubricant out of human semen, safely dismantling the flytrap, and how to create a dessert cake from [DATA EXPUNGED].

Why redact what the head is capable of doing, and then tell us what it's capable of doing? Also, an artificial intelligence that talks about how to make cake from unspeakable ingredients? It's been done.

Overall, not a bad entry, but it needs work. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The facility is mostly operated by humans.
However, this being implied, the whole place hasn't been explored yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Levels nine and ten are storage for raw ores and materials. Automated carts and arms can be seen transporting materials taken from the surface and transporting them to the elevators for melting down at the upper levels.

Wait, it takes supplies from the surface? In that case, wouldn't the containment procedure need to have a bit about preventing robots from getting out to scavenge for supplies? And wouldn't the factory have responded to having its supplies cut off?

Materials that have been located at this level include coal, iron ore, bauxite, silicon dioxide, copper ore, and unprocessed gemstones.

This suggests that the factory is surrounded by mining tunnels, from which it mines all of its supplies. If that's so, then the containment procedure would need to include something about preventing anyone from discovering these mining tunnels, like buying up all the mining rights to the surrounding areas so no one else tries to mine there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generally, no, because the object is rather predictable, and the behaviour of the machine is predictable.
However, it does cause one to wonder what this place's true capabilities are…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haven't read through the whole thing yet. But if it's "creating new robots in response to containment," wouldn't it be Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems well done if wordy.

+1
